name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱبْنَىْ ءَادَمَ بِٱلْحَقِّ إِذْ قَرَّبَا قُرْبَانًا فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمَا وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِ قَالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons. When the two of them offered an offering, it was accepted from one of them and not accepted from the other. [One of them] said, ‘Surely I will kill you.’ [The other one] said, ‘Allah accepts only from the Godwary. (27)
```

```arabic
لَئِنۢ بَسَطتَ إِلَىَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِى مَآ أَنَا۠ بِبَاسِطٍ يَدِىَ إِلَيْكَ لِأَقْتُلَكَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
```

```english
Even if you extend your hand toward me to kill me, I will not extend my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed, I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (28)
```

```arabic
إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوٓأَ بِإِثْمِى وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
I desire that you earn [the burden of] my sin and your sin, to become one of the inmates of the Fire, and such is the requital of the wrongdoers.’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُۥ نَفْسُهُۥ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُۥ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٣٠)
```

```english
So his soul prompted him to kill his brother, and he killed him, and thus became one of the losers. (30)
```

```arabic
فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ غُرَابًا يَبْحَثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُۥ كَيْفَ يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَةَ أَخِيهِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْغُرَابِ فَأُوَٰرِىَ سَوْءَةَ أَخِى ۖ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰدِمِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
Then Allah sent a crow, exploring in the ground, to show him how to bury the corpse of his brother. He said, ‘Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow and bury my brother’s corpse?’ Thus he became regretful. (31)
```

```arabic
مِنْ أَجْلِ ذَٰلِكَ كَتَبْنَا عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنَّهُۥ مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ (٣٢)
```

```english
That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter or corruption on the earth, is as though he had killed all mankind, and whoever saves a life is as though he had saved all mankind. Our apostles certainly brought them manifest signs, yet even after that, many of them commit excesses on the earth. (32)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses recount the story of the two sons of Adam, demonstrating how envy (الحسد) can drive a human being to the point of unjustly murdering his brother, thereby becoming one of the losers (الخاسرين) and feeling a regret (ندامة) that yields no benefit. In this sense, the passage is connected to the preceding discourse regarding the Children of Israel and their disdain for believing in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); for indeed, their refusal to accept the true call was born of nothing but envy and transgression (بغيا). Such is the nature of envy: it incites a person to kill his brother and then plunges him into a regret and sorrow from which there is no escape. Thus, they should take heed from this story and not persist so stubbornly in their envy and subsequent disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ابْنَيْ آدَمَ بِالْحَقِّ** — **"Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons"** (5:27). Recitation (التلاوة) is derived from "following" (التلو), and it refers to reading; it is so named because the one reciting an account (النبأ) brings forth its parts one following the other. An account (النبأ) is news that possesses utility and benefit. An offering (القربان) is that by which one seeks nearness (يتقرب به) to Allah, the Glorified, or to another; it is originally a verbal noun (مصدر) that is neither dualized nor pluralized. Acceptance (التقبل) is receiving something with increased care and attention toward the accepted object. The pronoun in His saying **عَلَيْهِمْ** ("to them") refers to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as they are the intended audience in the flow of the discourse.

The one named "Adam" here is the same Adam whom the Quran mentions as the father of humanity. Some exegetes have claimed he was a man from the Children of Israel whose two sons disputed over an offering they had presented, leading one to kill the other—Cain (قابيل) killing Abel (هابيل). They argue this is why Allah said after recounting the story: **مِنْ أَجْلِ ذلِكَ كَتَبْنا عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** — **"That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel..."** (5:32).

This view is invalid for several reasons. First, the Quran mentions no one by the name of Adam except the one it identifies as the father of humanity. Had the verse intended someone else, it would have been necessary to provide a contextual clue (قرينة) to prevent the story from becoming ambiguous.

Second, some specific details of the story, such as His saying: **فَبَعَثَ اللهُ غُراباً** — **"Then Allah sent a crow"** (5:31), only suit the initial state of humanity, when man lived with a primitive intellect (سذاجة من الفكر) and simple perception, acquiring information through his innate capacity (استعداده الجبلي) by accumulating experiences from particular events (الحوادث الجزئية) occurring one after another. The verse clearly indicates that the killer did not know that a dead body could be hidden by burying it in the earth. This characteristic fits the state of the son of Adam, the father of humanity, not a man from the Children of Israel, who were a people of civilization and urbanity (أهل حضارة ومدنية) according to their national history; such matters would certainly not have been hidden from any of them.

Third, regarding their argument that His saying after the completion of the story—**مِنْ أَجْلِ ذلِكَ كَتَبْنا عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** ("That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel")—serves as a response to an objection raised against the verse. The objection is: why was this decree specific to the Children of Israel when the logic of the story—which is what Allah decreed—applies to all humanity: that whoever kills a soul, it is as if he has killed all of mankind, and whoever saves a soul, it is as if he has saved all of mankind?

The proponent of that view answered by saying that the killer and the killed were not the sons of Adam, the father of humanity, such that their story would represent one of the primary events (الحوادث الأولية) of the human species and serve as a lesson for all who came after them. Rather, they were the sons of a man from the Children of Israel, and their account was part of their specific national history; thus, it was taken as a written lesson specifically for the Children of Israel.

However, this does not resolve the core of the problem. The question remains: the fact that killing one is like killing all, and saving one is like saving all, is a meaning linked to every murder occurring within this species, without being specific to one group over another. Countless murders occurred before the Children of Israel and before this specific murder referred to; why then was it predicated on a specific murder and decreed for a specific people?

Furthermore, had the matter been as they claim, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Whoever among *you* kills a soul..." so as to be specific to them. Then the question would return regarding this specification, despite its inherent lack of soundness.

The answer to the original problem is that what is contained in His saying: **أَنَّهُ مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ** — **"that whoever kills a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter..."** (5:32) is a profound wisdom (حكمة بالغة) and not merely a legislated ruling (حكم مشرع). The meaning of "decreeing it upon them" is the exposition of this wisdom to them, despite its benefit being general to them and others—much like the wisdoms and exhortations (المواعظ) explained in the Quran for the community of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while their benefit is not restricted to them. It was mentioned in the verse that He explained it to them because the verses are directed toward their admonition, awakening, and rebuke for their envy of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their persistence in enmity, inciting sedition, and initiating wars against the Muslims. For this reason, He followed His saying **مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً** ("whoever kills a soul") with His saying: **وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنا بِالْبَيِّناتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فِي الْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ** — **"Certainly Our apostles came to them with clear proofs, then indeed many of them, after that, are transgressors on the earth"** (5:32).

Moreover, the original story as they recounted it has no basis in narration (رواية) or history.

Thus, it becomes clear that His saying: **نَبَأَ ابْنَيْ آدَمَ بِالْحَقِّ** ("the account of Adam’s two sons truly") refers to the story of the two sons of Adam, the father of humanity. Restricting the speech with the phrase **بِالْحَقِّ** ("truly" or "with the truth")—which relates either to the "account" or to the command "relate"—is not devoid of an indication that the common version of the account circulating among them was not free from distortion (تحريف) or omission. This is indeed the case, as the story exists in the fourth chapter of Genesis in the Torah, yet it contains no mention of the sending of the crow and its scratching in the earth. Furthermore, that version of the story explicitly anthropomorphizes the Lord—exalted is He far above such things.

His saying: **إِذْ قَرَّبا قُرْباناً فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِما وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ الْآخَرِ** — **"when they both offered an offering, and it was accepted from one of them, and not accepted from the other"** (5:27). The context suggests that each of them presented something to the Lord to seek nearness. The word "offering" (قربان) was not dualized because it is originally a verbal noun that does not take dual or plural forms.

His saying: **قالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ قالَ إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** — **"One of them said, ‘I will surely kill you.’ The other said, ‘Allah accepts only from the God-conscious’"** (5:27). The first speaker is the killer and the second is the one killed. The context indicates they both knew that the offering of one had been accepted and the other's had not. As for how they knew this or by what means they inferred it, the verse is silent.

However, it is mentioned elsewhere in the speech of the Exalted that it was customary among previous nations, or specifically among the Children of Israel, for the acceptance of an offering to be manifested by fire consuming it. The Exalted said: **الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنا أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَنا بِقُرْبانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ النَّارُ قُلْ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِي بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَبِالَّذِي قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — **"Those who say, ‘Allah has pledged us not to believe in any apostle unless he brings us an offering consumed by fire.’ Say, ‘Apostles before me certainly brought you manifest signs and what you speak of. Then why did you kill them, if you are truthful?’"** (3:183). The concept of an offering (القربان) is well-known among the People of the Book to this day. [1] It is possible that the acceptance of the offering in this story was also of that nature, especially considering the story was presented to the People of the Book who held such beliefs. In any case, both the killer and the killed knew of the acceptance from one and the rejection from the other.

Furthermore, the context indicates that the one who said **لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ** ("I will surely kill you") was the one whose offering was not accepted, and that he said this out of personal envy (حسدا من نفسه), as there was no other reason; the one killed had committed no crime by his own choice that would warrant such a statement or a threat of death.

Thus, the killer's statement **لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ** was a threat of murder born of envy because the victim's offering was accepted while his own was not. The victim's response: **إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** ("Allah accepts only from the God-conscious")—along with the rest of what Allah recounted from him—was a reply to the killer's statement. He first reminded him that the matter of the offering's acceptance or rejection was not of his own doing, nor was it a crime on his part; rather, the fault lay with the killer for failing to be God-conscious (لم يتق الله), and thus Allah requited him by not accepting his offering.

Secondly, he stated that even if the killer intended to murder him and stretched out his hand to do so, he would not stretch out his own hand to kill him, due to his godwariness (تقواه) and fear of Allah, the Glorified. Rather, in such a scenario, he intended for the killer to return bearing the sin of the victim and his own sin, thereby becoming one of the inmates of the Fire—and that is the reward of the wrongdoers (الظالمين).

His saying: **إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** ("Allah accepts only from the God-conscious") is framed as a restriction of individuation (قصر الإفراد) to indicate that acceptance does not encompass the offerings of both the God-conscious and the non-God-conscious together. Or it is a restriction of reversal (قصر القلب), as if the killer presumed his offering would be accepted instead of the victim's, thinking that the matter did not revolve around godwariness (التقوى), or that Allah was not aware of the reality of the situation—that He might be confused by the matter as a human might be.

In this speech is an exposition of the reality of the acceptance of acts of worship (العبادات) and offerings, as well as an admonition and a proclamation regarding murder, injustice, and envy. It affirms divine requital (المجازاة الإلهية) and that this is a necessary consequence of the Lordship (الربوبية) of the Lord of all the worlds. For Lordship is not complete without a precise system (نظام متقن) among the parts of the universe that leads to the evaluation of deeds by the scale of justice (ميزان العدل) and the requital of injustice with painful punishment, so that the wrongdoer might be deterred from his injustice or receive the recompense he has prepared for himself, which is the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَئِنْ بَسَطْتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِي ما أَنَا بِباسِطٍ يَدِيَ إِلَيْكَ** — **"Even if you extend your hand toward me to kill me, I will not extend my hand toward you to kill you"** (5:28). The *lam* is for an oath (للقسم). "Extending the hand" is a metaphor (كناية) for taking the preliminary steps toward murder and employing its means. In the response to the condition, he used a negation of a nominal sentence, employing the active participle (**بِباسِطٍ**) rather than a verb, and emphasized the negation with the particle *ba*, then further emphasized the speech with an oath. All of this indicates that he was leagues away from any desire to kill his brother; he did not intend it, nor did it even cross his mind.

He reinforced all of this by providing the reason for his claim **ما أَنَا بِباسِطٍ يَدِيَ** ("I will not extend my hand") with his saying: **إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ** — **"Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds"** (5:28). For the God-conscious, remembering their Lord—Who is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds, Who requites every sin with its subsequent punishment—awakens within their souls the instinct of the fear of Allah (الخوف من الله), preventing them from committing an injustice that would lead them to destruction.

Then he mentioned the interpretation (تأويل) of his saying: **لَئِنْ بَسَطْتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِي ما أَنَا بِباسِطٍ يَدِيَ** ("Even if you extend your hand..."), meaning the reality of what he had informed him of. The gist is that, in such a scenario, the matter revolves between him killing his brother—thereby becoming the wrongdoer bearing the sin and entering the Fire—or his brother killing him, in which case the brother would be the one in that state. He does not choose to kill his wrongdoing brother over his own salvation while he himself is not a wrongdoer; rather, he chooses that his wrongdoing brother suffer the wretchedness of the act of murder while he himself attains felicity as one who is not a wrongdoer. This is the meaning of his saying: **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ** ("Indeed I desire..."), where "desire" is a metaphor for "choice" (الاختيار) in the event that the situation is narrowed down to these two options.

Thus, the verse, in being an interpretation of his saying **لَئِنْ بَسَطْتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ** ("Even if you extend your hand"), is like what occurred in the story of Moses and his companion when the latter killed a youth they met. Moses objected, saying: **أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْساً زَكِيَّةً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً نُكْراً** — **"Did you kill an innocent soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter? Certainly you have done a monstrous thing!"** (18:74). His companion then informed him of the interpretation of what he had done, saying: **وَأَمَّا الْغُلامُ فَكانَ أَبَواهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينا أَنْ يُرْهِقَهُما طُغْياناً وَكُفْراً فَأَرَدْنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُما رَبُّهُما خَيْراً مِنْهُ زَكاةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْماً** — **"As for the boy, his parents were faithful [persons], and we feared he would overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaithfulness. So we desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in purity and closer in affection"** (18:80-81).

The one killed "desired"—meaning he chose—death with felicity (السعادة), even if it necessitated his brother's wretchedness due to the brother's own evil choice, over life with wretchedness and joining the party of the wrongdoers. Similarly, the companion of Moses chose the death of the youth with felicity, even if it necessitated grief and sorrow for his parents, over his living and becoming a rebellious disbeliever who would go astray himself and lead his parents astray, while Allah would compensate them with one better in purity and closer in affection.

The man—meaning the murdered son of Adam—was among the God-conscious (المتقين) who possess knowledge of Allah (العلماء بالله). As for his being among the God-conscious, it is due to his saying: **إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** ("Allah accepts only from the God-conscious"), which contains a claim to godwariness that Allah affirmed by narrating it without rejection. As for his being among those who know Allah, it is due to his saying: **إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ** ("Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds"). He claimed the fear of Allah, and Allah affirmed it from him. The Exalted has said: **إِنَّما يَخْشَى اللهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ** — **"Only those of Allah’s servants who possess knowledge fear Him"** (35:28). Thus, Allah's narration of his saying **إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ** and His affirmation of it serves as a description of him as possessing knowledge (العلم), just as the companion of Moses was described with knowledge when He said: **وَعَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً** — **"and whom We had taught a knowledge from Our own"** (18:65).

It is sufficient evidence of his knowledge to consider the profound wisdom and fair admonition (الموعظة الحسنة) with which he addressed his transgressing brother. He explained, out of the purity of his nature (طهارة طينته) and the clarity of his primordial nature (الفطرة), that humanity would increase in number and then their groups would differ according to human disposition: some would be God-conscious and others wrongdoers. He explained that they all, and all the worlds, have one Lord Who owns them and manages their affairs, and that part of this precise management (التدبير المتقن) is that He loves and approves of justice and excellence (الإحسان), while He hates and is displeased by injustice and aggression. The necessary consequence of this is the obligation of godwariness and the fear of Allah upon man, which is religion (الدين). Thus, there are acts of obedience and offerings, and there are sins and injustices. Acts of obedience and offerings are only accepted if they stem from godwariness, while sins and injustices are burdens borne by the wrongdoer. A further consequence is that there must be another realm (نشأة أخرى) in which requital occurs, and the reward of the wrongdoers is the Fire.

These—as you can see—are the foundations of religious gnosis (المعارف الدينية) and the sum of the sciences of the Origin and the Return (المبدأ والمعاد). This righteous servant poured them forth abundantly for his ignorant brother, who did not even know that a thing could be hidden from sight by burial until he learned it from the crow. Furthermore, he did not say to his brother when he spoke to him: "If you want to kill me, I will throw myself before you and not defend myself or guard against being killed." He only said: "I would not be the one to kill you."

Nor did he say: "I desire to be killed by your hand in any case so that you may be a wrongdoer and an inmate of the Fire." For inciting someone's misguidance and wretchedness in life is an injustice and a deviation in the law of the primordial nature (شريعة الفطرة), not specific to any one revealed law over another. Rather, he said: "I desire that and choose it *on the condition* that you extend your hand to kill me."

From this, the refutation of the objections raised against the story becomes clear. One objection was that just as the killer was excessive in injustice and transgression, the victim was deficient in passivity and submissiveness (الانظلام), as he did not address him or confront him with self-defense, but rather surrendered himself and complied with the brother's will to kill him when he said: **لَئِنْ بَسَطْتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ** ("Even if you extend your hand...").

The refutation is that he did not say: "I will not defend myself and I will leave you to do what you want with me." He only said: "I do not desire to kill you." It is not mentioned in the verse that he was killed without defending himself while being aware of the act; perhaps he was killed by stealth (غيلة), or he was killed while defending himself or taking precautions.

Similarly, another objection was that he mentioned his desire to enable his brother to kill him so that the brother would suffer eternal punishment, thereby making himself happy, when he said: **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ** — **"I desire that you should bear my sin and your own sin, and so become among the inmates of the Fire"** (5:29). This is likened to some overly ascetic worshippers who believe their duty is only to practice renunciation and worship, and if a wrongdoer oppresses them, the wrongdoer bears the burden of his injustice, while they have no duty of defense except patience and seeking reward from Allah. This is ignorance, for it constitutes aiding in sin (الإعانة على الإثم), which necessitates that both the aider and the aided share in the sin, rather than the wrongdoer bearing both alone.

The refutation is that his saying **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ** ("I desire that you should bear my sin and your own sin") is a statement based on a condition, in the sense previously explained.

Some have answered these two objections with weak arguments that are not worth mentioning.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ** — **"I desire that you should bear my sin and your own sin, and so become among the inmates of the Fire"** (5:29). That is, you return with my sin and your sin, as some have interpreted it. Al-Raghib said in his *Mufradat*: "The root of **البواء** (returning/bearing) is the equality of parts in a place, the opposite of *al-nabwah* (rejection/mismatch), which is the incompatibility of parts. It is said: 'a place of *bawa'*' if it is not unsuitable for settling. And 'I prepared (*bawwa'tu*) a place for him' means I leveled it. So *tabu'* (you bear/return)..." until he said: "And His saying **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ** means you remain in this state." He said: "I denied its falsehood and bore (*bu'tu*) its truth." End quote. Based on this, interpreting it as "returning" is an interpretation by the necessary implication of the meaning.

The meaning of His saying **أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ** ("that you should bear my sin and your own sin") is that the sin of the one unjustly killed is transferred to his killer, added to the sin the killer already possessed, so both sins accumulate upon him. The victim meets Allah, the Glorified, with no sin upon him. This is the apparent meaning of His saying **أَنْ تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ**, and narrations have been transmitted to this effect, and rational consideration (الاعتبار العقلي) supports it.

A portion of the discussion on this was presented in the discourse on the "ruling of deeds" in the second volume of this book.

The objection that this necessitates the possibility of punishing a person for the sin of another—which reason judges to be impossible, and Allah has said: **أَلَّا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** — **"that no bearer shall bear another’s burden"** (53:38)—is refuted by the fact that this is not a judgment of theoretical reason (العقل النظري) such that it would be deemed impossible to occur. Rather, it is among the judgments of practical reason (العقل العملي) which follow the interests of human society in their establishment and change. It is permissible for society to consider an act originating from one person as an act originating from another, recording it against him and holding him accountable for it. Or, an act originating from him may be considered as not originating from him. For example, if one kills a person and society had rights over the victim that it was supposed to collect from him, it is permissible for society to collect those rights from the killer. Similarly, if one transgresses against society by rebellion, corruption, and disturbing public security, society may consider all the transgressor's good deeds as if they never existed, and so on.

In these cases and their like, society does not see the evil deeds that originated from the oppressed except as burdens (أوزارا) for the oppressor. The bearer only bears her own burden, not the burden of another, because she has taken possession of it from the other through the injustice and evil she inflicted upon him—similar to how a person buys what another owns for a price. Just as the actions of the new owner are not prevented by the fact that the first owner possessed the item for a time—since it has been transferred to another as property—similarly, His saying **أَلَّا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** ("that no bearer shall bear another’s burden") does not prevent holding the killing soul accountable for a sin simply because the bearing soul was someone else for a time. Nor does His saying **لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** remain without benefit or effect due to the permissibility of the transfer of a burden through a new cause, just as the saying of him (peace be upon him): "The property of a Muslim man is not lawful except with his heart's consent" does not remain without benefit by permitting the transfer of ownership through sale and the like.

Some exegetes have mentioned that the meaning of His saying **بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ** ("my sin and your sin") is: the sin of killing me if you kill me, and your sin that you had committed before that, as narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, Ibn Abbas, and others. Or that it means: the sin of killing me and your sin for which your offering was not accepted, as narrated from al-Jubba'i and al-Zajjaj. Or that it means: the sin of killing me and your sin which is the killing of all people, as narrated from others.

These are views they have mentioned, yet none of them has evidence from the wording, nor does rational consideration support them. Furthermore, the contrast between the two sins—while both belong to the killer—and then naming one as the sin of the victim and the other as the sin of the killer, is devoid of justification.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُ نَفْسُهُ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ** — **"Then his soul prompted him to kill his brother, and he killed him, and thus became one of the losers"** (5:30). Al-Raghib said in his *Mufradat*: "**الطوع** (compliance) is submission, and its opposite is compulsion. **الطاعة** (obedience) is similar, but it is most often used in following what is commanded and adhering to what is prescribed. His saying **فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُ نَفْسُهُ** is like saying his companion made it easy for him, submitted to him, or enticed him. *Tawwa'at* (prompted/made compliant) is more emphatic than *ata'at* (obeyed). 'His soul made it compliant for him' is in contrast to their saying: 'his soul turned away from such-and-such.'" End quote in summary. His meaning is not that *tawwa'at* incorporates the meaning of "submitted" or "enticed," but rather that the form *tatwi'* indicates gradualness, whereas *ita'ah* indicates a sudden act, as is common in the patterns of *if'al* and *taf'il*. Thus, *tatwi'* in the verse refers to the soul's gradual approach toward the act through whisper after whisper and impulse after impulse, submitting to it until complete obedience is achieved. Thus, His saying **قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ** ("the killing of his brother") places the commanded object in the position of the command, as in their saying: "he obeyed such-and-such" in place of "he obeyed the command for such-and-such."

It has also been said that *tawwa'at* means "beautified" (زينت), so **قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ** is the direct object. It has also been said it means "complied with" (طاوعت), meaning his soul complied with him in the killing of his brother, so "killing" is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition. The meaning of the verse is clear.

It might be inferred from His saying **فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ** ("and thus became [literally: became in the morning] one of the losers") that he killed him at night. Regarding this, it has been said that *asbaha*—which is the opposite of *amsa* (became in the evening)—although it conveys that meaning according to its root, is used in Arabic convention to mean "became" (صار) without regard for its original derivation. In the Quran, there is much of this sort, such as His saying: **فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْواناً** — **"and by His blessing you became brothers"** (3:103) and His saying: **فَيُصْبِحُوا عَلى ما أَسَرُّوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ نادِمِينَ** — **"and then become regretful for what they had kept secret in their hearts"** (5:52). Thus, there is no way to establish that the original meaning is intended here.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَبَعَثَ اللهُ غُراباً يَبْحَذُ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُ كَيْفَ يُوارِي سَوْأَةَ أَخِيهِ** — **"Then Allah sent a crow, scratching the earth, to show him how to conceal the corpse of his brother"** (5:31). **البحث** (scratching/searching) is seeking something in the soil; then it is said: "I searched (*bahathtu*) for the matter such-and-such," as in *al-Majma'*. **المواراة** (concealing) is covering; from it comes *al-tawari* for hiding, and *al-wara'* for what is behind a thing. **السوأة** (corpse/shameful thing) is that which a human finds loathsome. **الويل** (woe) is destruction. **يا ويلتا** ("Woe to me!") is a word said at the time of destruction. **العجز** (inability) is the opposite of capability (الاستطاعة).

The verse, by its context, indicates that the killer had remained for a time in a state of confusion regarding his affair, fearing that others would find out, and not knowing what trick to use so they would not discover the body, until Allah sent the crow. Had the sending of the crow, its scratching, and his killing of his brother been close in time, there would be no reason for his saying: **يا وَيْلَتى أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هذَا الْغُرابِ** — **"Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow?"** (5:31).

Similarly, what is understood from the context is that the crow buried something in the earth after scratching; for the apparent meaning of the speech is that the crow intended to show the *how* of concealment, not the *how* of scratching. Mere scratching would not have taught him the method of concealment, as he was in such a state of simple understanding that his mind had not yet moved to the meaning of scratching; how then would it move from scratching to concealment when there is no necessary link between them? He only moved to the meaning of concealment by seeing that the crow scratched in the earth and then buried something in it.

The crow, among birds, has the habit of storing some of what it hunts for itself by burying it in the earth. While some birds that feed on seeds and the like might scratch in the earth, it is to obtain things like seeds and worms, not for burial and storage.

What was mentioned previously regarding returning the subject pronoun in **لِيُرِيَهُ** ("to show him") to the crow is the apparent meaning of the speech, as it is the nearest referent. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified; there is no harm in this, though it is not devoid of some distance. The meaning is correct in both cases. As for his saying: **قالَ يا وَيْلَتى أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هذَا الْغُرابِ** ("He said, ‘Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow?'"), he said this because he found the trick of the crow for concealment to be simple. He found himself capable of doing what the crow did—scratching and then using it as a means to concealment—due to the clear connection between scratching and concealment. At that moment, he felt sorrow for the benefit he had missed and regretted his neglect in thinking of a means to concealment until it became clear to him that scratching was the proximate means to it. He expressed this regret by saying: **يا وَيْلَتى أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هذَا الْغُرابِ فَأُوارِيَ سَوْأَةَ أَخِي** ("Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow and conceal my brother’s corpse?"). This is a dialogue occurring between him and himself by way of a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري). The implication is that he asks himself reproachfully: "Were you unable to be like this crow and conceal your brother's corpse?" and the answer is: "No." Then he asks a second rhetorical question: "Then why were you heedless of that and did not employ this means despite its clarity, making yourself wretched during this period for no reason?" There is no answer to this question, and in it lies the regret (الندامة). Regret is a specific spiritual effect in a human and an internal pain that occurs when he witnesses his neglect of one of the causes leading to the loss of a benefit or the occurrence of a harm. If you wish, you could say it is the human's reaction arising from his memory of his neglect in utilizing one of the possibilities (الإمكانات).

This is the state of a human when he commits injustices that he hates for people to discover. These are matters that society, with its current system whose parts are interconnected, does not accept. The effect of these matters that contradict the system must appear, even if they are hidden from people at their first occurrence. The wrongdoing criminal wants to force the system to accept him, but it will not accept him—just as a person might eat or drink poison and want his digestive system to digest it, but it will not digest it. Although it may enter his interior, it has an appointment it will not fail and a vantage point it will not bypass; indeed, your Lord is ever on the watch.

At that point, the human's deficiency in management (نقص تدبيره) regarding what he should have monitored and observed becomes apparent to him, and he regrets it. If he were to return and fix this one thing, another would spoil, and the matter continues like that until Allah exposes him before all witnesses.

It has become clear from the preceding exposition that His saying: **فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ النَّادِمِينَ** — **"and he became regretful"** (5:31) is an allusion to his regret over not concealing his brother's corpse. It might also be possible to say that it refers to his regret over the original act of murder, and this is not far-fetched.

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of Sensation and Thinking)

This portion of the story of the two sons of Adam—namely His saying: **فَبَعَثَ اللهُ غُراباً يَبْحَثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُ كَيْفَ يُوارِي سَوْأَةَ أَخِيهِ قالَ يا وَيْلَتى أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هذَا الْغُرابِ فَأُوارِيَ سَوْأَةَ أَخِي فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ النَّادِمِينَ**—is a unique verse in the Quran with no parallel of its kind. It represents the human state in benefiting from sensation (الحس), and how he acquires the properties of things through sensation, then employs thinking (التفكر) about them to achieve his goals and purposes in life. This is in accordance with what scientific research dictates: that human sciences and knowledge terminate at sensation, contrary to those who argue for recollection (التذكر) and innate knowledge (العلم الفطري).

To clarify: if you examine the scientific images (الصور العلمية) a human possesses—whether they be particular or universal concepts (تصور) or judgments (تصديق), and whatever the nature of his knowledge and perceptions—you will find that even the most ignorant of people and those weakest in understanding and thought possess many images and vast knowledge that can hardly be enumerated; indeed, none can count them but the Lord of all the worlds.

What is witnessed regarding them, despite their abundance and being beyond enumeration, is that they continue to increase and grow throughout human life in this world. If we were to trace back, we would find them decreasing and decreasing until they reach zero, and the human returns to having no actual knowledge at all. The Exalted said: **عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** — **"taught man what he did not know"** (96:5).

The verse does not mean that He, the Exalted, teaches him what he did not know, while as for what he already knew, he is independent of his Lord's teaching. For it is necessary that knowledge in a human, whatever it may be, is for his guidance (لهدايته) toward that by which he perfects his existence and benefits in his life. That toward which non-living things move by natural impulses, living beings—including humans—move and are guided toward by the light of knowledge. Thus, knowledge is one of the instances of guidance (الهدى).

Allah, the Glorified, has attributed absolute guidance to Himself, saying: **الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى** — **"He who gave everything its creation and then guided it"** (20:50), and He said: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فهدى** — **"who created and proportioned, who determined and guided"** (87:2-3). He also said, regarding one aspect of guidance through sensation and thought: **أَمَّنْ يَهْدِيكُمْ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ** — **"Who guides you in the darkness of land and sea"** (26:63). A portion of the discourse on the meaning of guidance has passed in previous discussions. In short, since every knowledge is guidance and every guidance is from Allah, every knowledge of man is by His teaching.

Close to His saying **عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** ("taught man what he did not know") is His saying: **وَاللهُ أَخْرَجَكُمْ مِنْ بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ** — **"Allah has brought you forth from your mothers’ bellies while you did not know anything. He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts"** (16:78).

Contemplation of the human state and reflection on the noble verses indicate that human theoretical knowledge (العلم النظري)—meaning knowledge of the properties of things and the subsequent rational sciences—begins from sensation. Allah teaches him the properties of things through this path, as indicated by His saying: **فَبَعَثَ اللهُ غُراباً يَبْحَثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُ كَيْفَ يُوارِي سَوْأَةَ أَخِيهِ** ("Then Allah sent a crow... to show him how to conceal...").

The attribution of sending the crow to show the method of concealment to Allah, the Glorified, is the very same as the attribution of teaching the method of concealment to Him, the Exalted. The crow, though it does not perceive that Allah, the Glorified, is the one who sent it, and similarly the son of Adam, who did not know there was a Manager managing his thinking and learning—and the causality of the crow and its scratching relative to his learning was, according to outward appearance, a coincidental causality (سببية اتفاقية) like all other coincidental causes through which man learns the ways of managing his livelihood and his return—yet Allah, the Glorified, is the one who created man and led him to the perfection of knowledge for the purpose of his life. He organized the universe in a way that leads him to perfection through knowledge by types of contact and collision occurring between him and the parts of the universe. Thus, man learns that by which he achieves his goals and purposes in life; so Allah, the Glorified, is the one who sends the crow and others to an act by which man learns something, so He is the Teacher of man.

There are parallels to this meaning in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَما عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللهُ** — **"and [the prey of] the hunting dogs you have trained, teaching them out of what Allah has taught you"** (5:4). He counted what they knew and taught as being from what Allah taught them, even though they learned it from other people or innovated it through their own thoughts. And His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ اللهُ** — **"Be God-conscious, and Allah will teach you"** (2:282), even though they were learning it from the Messenger. And His saying: **وَلا يَأْبَ كاتِبٌ أَنْ يَكْتُبَ كَما عَلَّمَهُ اللهُ** — **"and let no scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him"** (2:282), even though the scribe learned what he knew by learning from another scribe like him. However, all of these are intended matters in creation and management (التدبير). Whatever benefit of knowledge is obtained through these causes, by which man is perfected, Allah, the Glorified, is his teacher through these causes, just as a human teacher teaches through speech and instruction, and a human scribe teaches another through speech and the pen, for example.

This is the way in all that is attributed to Him, the Exalted, in the world of causes (عالم الأسباب). Allah, the Exalted, is its Creator, and between Him and His creature are causes which are the causes according to outward appearance, and they are tools and instruments for the existence of the thing. If you wish, you could say they are among the conditions for the existence of the thing whose existence is linked from all its sides and aspects to the causes. Among the conditions for the existence of Zayd "who was born to 'Amr and Hind" is that 'Amr and Hind precede him, along with marriage and union between them; otherwise, the supposed Zayd would not exist. Among the conditions for "seeing with the seeing eye" is that there be a seeing eye before it, and so on.

Whoever presumes that he declares the oneness of Allah (يوحد الله) by negating and abolishing causes, imagining that this is more effective in affirming His absolute power and negating inability from Him, and presumes that affirming the necessity of the intervention of causes is a claim that He, the Exalted, is compelled to follow a specific path in creation and lacks choice (الاختيار)—such a person has contradicted himself without realizing it.

In short, Allah, the Glorified, is the one who taught man the properties of things that his senses grasp in some way; He taught them to him through the path of the senses, then He subjected to him all that is in the earth and the heavens. The Exalted said: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً مِنْهُ** — **"He has disposed for you[r benefit] whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth; all is from Him"** (45:13).

This subjection (التسخير) is only so that he may employ a type of disposal (تصرف) over them to reach his goals and hopes in life; that is, He made them linked to his existence so he may benefit from them, and He made him a thinking being who is guided to the method of disposal, use, and employment. Evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَالْفُلْكَ تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ** — **"Have you not regarded that Allah has disposed for you[r benefit] whatever is on the earth, and the ships that sail at sea by His command?"** (22:65), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ** — **"and He has made for you ships and livestock such as you ride"** (43:12), and His saying, the Exalted: **عَلَيْها وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ** — **"and you are carried on them and on ships"** (40:80), and other similar verses. Look at the language of the verses: how He attributed the making of the ships to Allah, the Glorified, while they are of human manufacture; then He attributed the carrying to Him, the Exalted, while it is the work of the ships and livestock; and He attributed their sailing in the sea to His command, while it is based on the flow of the sea or the blowing of the wind or steam and the like. He called all of that a subjection from Him for the human, because his will possesses a type of authority (حكومة) over the ships and the livestock that resemble them, and over the earth and the heaven, leading them to the ends desired by him.

In short, He, the Glorified, gave him thought (الفكر) based on sensation (الحس) so he may employ it for his destined perfection through his intellectual sciences (علوم فكرية) that operate in the ontological realm (التكوينيات)—meaning the theoretical sciences.

The Exalted said: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — **"and He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts, so that you may give thanks"** (16:78). As for the practical sciences (العلوم العملية)—which are those that operate in what *ought* to be done and what *ought not* to be done—they are only by inspiration (إلهام) from Allah, the Glorified, without sensation or theoretical reason producing them. The Exalted said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها** — **"by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness: one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it is certainly a failure"** (91:7-10). And He said: **فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — **"So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion"** (30:30). Thus, He counted the knowledge of what ought to be done—which is the good deed (الحسنة)—and what ought not to be done—which is the evil deed (السيئة)—as being among what is obtained through divine inspiration, which is the casting into the heart.

Thus, all the knowledge that a human obtains is divine guidance (هداية إلهية) and by divine guidance, though it differs according to type: what pertains to the properties of external things, the path by which Allah, the Glorified, guides the human is the path of sensation. What pertains to the universal intellectual sciences (العلوم الكلية الفكرية), they are by divine granting and subjection, without the existence of sensation nullifying them or the human being independent of them in any state. What pertains to the practical sciences related to the righteousness or corruption of deeds, and what is godwariness (تقوى) or wickedness (فجور), they are by divine inspiration through casting into the hearts and knocking on the door of the primordial nature (الفطرة).

The third category, which traces back in its origin to divine inspiration, only succeeds in its work and is complete in its effect if the second category is sound and grows upon health and uprightness—just as reason (العقل) only remains upright in its work if the human remains upright in his godwariness and his primordial religion. The Exalted said: **وَما يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ** — **"and no one takes admonition except those who possess intellect"** (3:7). And the Exalted said: **وَما يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُنِيبُ** — **"and no one takes admonition except him who turns [to Allah] penitently"** (40:13). And the Exalted said: **وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْئِدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصارَهُمْ كَما لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — **"We shall turn their hearts and their sights, just as they did not believe in it the first time"** (6:110). And the Exalted said: **وَمَنْ يَرْغَبُ عَنْ مِلَّةِ إِبْراهِيمَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ** — **"And who is it that turns away from the creed of Abraham except one who befools himself?"** (2:130). That is, no one leaves the requirements of the primordial nature except one whose intellect has become corrupted and thus follows a path other than its own.

Rational consideration supports this necessary link between reason and godwariness. For if a human is afflicted in his theoretical faculty (قوته النظرية) such that he does not perceive the truth as truth or does not perceive falsehood as falsehood, how can he be inspired with the necessity of this or the avoidance of that? Like one who sees that there is nothing beyond the immediate material life; such a person is not inspired with religious godwariness, which is the best provision for the afterlife.

Similarly, if a human's primordial religion is corrupted and he does not provide himself with religious godwariness, his internal sensory faculties—such as desire, anger, love, hatred, and others—will not be balanced. With the disorder of these faculties, the theoretical faculty of perception (قوة الإدراك النظرية) will not perform its work in a satisfactory manner.

The Quranic expositions follow this course in spreading religious gnosis and teaching people beneficial knowledge. They observe the aforementioned paths designated for obtaining information. What pertains to particulars (الجزئيات) that have properties susceptible to sensation, they refer to the senses, as in verses containing phrases like: "Have you not seen?", "Do they not see?", "Have you regarded?", "Do you not perceive?", and others. What pertains to rational universals (الكليات العقلية) related to universal material matters or those beyond the world of visibility (عالم الشهادة), they consider reason (العقل) in them with a decisive consideration, even if they are absent from sensation and outside the realm of matter and material things—as is the case with most verses related to the Origin and the Return containing phrases like: "for a people who exercise intellect (يعقلون)", "for a people who reflect (يتفكرون)", "for a people who take admonition (يتذكرون)", "they understand (يفقهون)", and others. What pertains to practical propositions (القضايا العملية) that have a connection to good and evil, and the beneficial and the harmful in action, godwariness, and wickedness, they rely on divine inspiration by mentioning that which, upon remembering it, the human feels his internal inspiration—as in verses containing phrases like: "that is better for you", "indeed his heart is sinful", "in both of them is sin", "and sin and aggression without right", "indeed Allah does not guide", and others. You should contemplate them.

From here, it becomes clear, first: that the Noble Quran deems the path of the sensationalists (الحسيين) to be erroneous—those who rely on sensation and experiment (التجربة), negating purely rational judgments in scientific research. This is because the first thing the Quran emphasizes in its exposition is the matter of the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, exalted is His Name; then all the true gnoses (المعارف الحقيقية) that it explains and calls toward return to it and are built upon it.

It is well known that monotheism is the issue furthest removed from sensation, most distinct from matter, and most connected to purely rational judgments.

The Quran explains that these true gnoses are from the primordial nature (الفطرة). He said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ** — **"So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation"** (30:30). That is, the human creation is a type of origination (إيجاد) that necessitates these sciences and perceptions. There is no meaning to "altering creation" unless the alteration itself is also from creation and origination. As for altering absolute origination—meaning the nullification of the judgment of reality—no meaning can be conceived for it. Man will never be able—far be it—to nullify his primordial sciences and follow in life a path other than theirs at all. As for the witnessed deviation from the judgments of the primordial nature, it is not a nullification of its judgment, but rather an employment of it in an improper manner—similar to how it might happen that an archer does not hit the target in his shot; the instrument of shooting and its other conditions are naturally set for hitting the target, but the usage causes it to fall into error. Knives, saws, drills, needles, and their like, if they are installed in machines in a crooked manner, they perform the very work they were fashioned for—cutting, sawing, drilling, and so on—but not in the intended way. As for deviating from the primordial act, such as sewing with the sawing of a saw—by having the saw replace the act of the needle with its own act, placing sewing in the position of sawing—that is impossible.

This is apparent to whoever contemplates the general arguments the people have used for the correctness of their path, such as their saying: "Purely rational research and syllogisms composed of premises far from sensation frequently fall into error, as indicated by the abundance of differences in purely rational issues; thus, one should not rely on them because the soul does not find tranquility in them."

And their saying in arguing for the correctness of the path of sensation and experiment: "Sensation is an instrument for attaining the properties of things by necessity. If an effect is sensed in one of the subjects under specific conditions, and then the observation of the effect is repeated with those same conditions preserved without failure or variation, this reveals that this effect is a property of the subject and not by chance, because chance (الاتفاق) never persists."

Both arguments, as you can see, were driven to establish the obligation of relying on sensation and experiment and rejecting purely rational conduct, while the premises taken in both of them are purely rational premises (مقدمات عقلية) outside of sensation and experiment. Then, by taking these rational premises, they intended to nullify taking them! This is what was previously mentioned: that the primordial nature will never be nullified, but man only errs in the method of its employment.

More heinous than that is the employment of experiment in identifying legislated rulings (الأحكام المشرعة) and established laws—as if a ruling is established and then implemented among people to test the goodness of its effect through statistics and the like; if the goodness of the result prevails in the cases of its implementation, it is taken as a fixed, ongoing ruling, otherwise it is cast aside and another is taken similarly, and so on. Similar to this is establishing a ruling by analogy (القياس) or juristic preference (الاستحسان). [1]

The Quran nullifies all of that by establishing that legislated rulings are primordial and clear, and that general godwariness and wickedness are inspired and known, and that their details are among what must be taken from the side of revelation (الوحي). The Exalted said: **وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ** — **"Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge"** (17:36). And He said: **وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ** — **"and do not follow in Satan’s footsteps"** (2:168). The Quran calls the legislated law (الشريعة المشرعة) "truth" (حقا). The Exalted said: **أَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** — **"and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed"** (2:213). And He said: **وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً** — **"and indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth"** (53:28). And how could it be a substitute when in following it lies the fear of falling into the danger of falsehood, which is misguidance (الضلال)?

***

[1] As for juristic analogy (القياس الفقهي), juristic preference (الاستحسان), and what is called "the scent of jurisprudence" (شم الفقاهة), they are indicators for discovering the ruling, not for establishing it, and the discussion of them is left to the discipline of the Principles of Jurisprudence (أصول الفقه).

He said: "**So what is there after the truth except error?**" (10:32), and He said: "**Indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead**" (16:37). This means that error (الضلال) is not a suitable path to lead man to goodness and felicity. Thus, whoever seeks to use falsehood (الباطل) as a means to reach truth (الحق), or injustice (الظلم) to reach justice (العدل), or an evil deed (السيئة) to reach a righteous deed (الحسنة), or profligacy (الفجور) to reach godwariness (التقوى), has missed the path. Such a person expects from the system of creation and existence (الصنع والإيجاد)—which is the foundation for divine laws and legislations—something that it will never permit. Were this possible, it would apply to the properties of opposite things, and one of two opposites would take upon itself the function and effect that belongs to the other.

Similarly, the Quran invalidates the path of "recollection" (التذكر) — *the Platonic theory that learning is merely remembering innate knowledge* — which involves the negation of the intellectual and scientific process and the dismissal of the logic of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. We have already discussed this previously.

Likewise, the Quran prohibits people from reflecting without the accompaniment of godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah, the Glorified; this has also been discussed previously in general. For this reason, you see that when the Quran teaches the laws of religion (شرائع الدين), it couples the ruling it explains with ethical virtues and praiseworthy traits. By remembering these, the instinct of godwariness (التقوى) is awakened in man, enabling him to better understand and comprehend the ruling. Consider this in verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **And when you divorce women and they complete their term, do not prevent them from marrying their [prospective] husbands if they agree together honorably. That is an admonition for those of you who believe in Allah and the Last Day. That is more decent for you and purer, and Allah knows and you do not know.** (2:232); and His saying, the Exalted: **Fight them until there is no [more] strife, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. But if they desist, there shall be no aggression except against the wrongdoers.** (2:193); and His saying, the Exalted: **Maintain the prayer. Indeed the prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater. And Allah knows whatever you do.** (29:45).

***

His saying, the Exalted: **From that account We ordained for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul, without [its being retaliation for] a soul or for corruption [done] on the earth, it is as though he had killed all mankind, and whoever keeps it alive, it is as though he had kept all mankind alive.** (5:32). In *al-Majma'*, it is stated: "The word *ajal* (أجل) in the language means a crime (الجناية)." Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "*al-Ajal* (الأجل) is a crime whose consequences are feared later (*ājilan*); thus every *ajal* is a crime, but not every crime is an *ajal*. It is said: 'I did that because of him' (*min ajlihi*)."

Then it came to be used for causation (التعليل). It is said: "I did it because of (*min ajli*) such-and-such," meaning that such-and-such was the reason for my action. Perhaps the use of the word for causation began first in the context of crimes and offenses, as when we say: "So-and-so behaved badly, and because of that (*min ajli dhalik*) I disciplined him with a beating," meaning my beating arose from his crime and offense, which was his bad behavior. Later, it was used as a general causal term, so it is said: "I visit you because of (*min ajli*) my love for you."

The apparent context indicates that the demonstrative "that" in His saying "**From that account**" (مِنْ أَجْلِ ذلِكَ) refers to the story of the two sons of Adam mentioned in the previous verses. That is, the occurrence of that tragic event was the reason for Our ordaining for the Children of Israel such-and-such. It has also been suggested that His saying "**From that account**" is connected to His saying in the previous verse: "**so he became one of the regretful**" (2:31), meaning that [the murder] was the cause of his regret. While this view is not inherently far-fetched—similar to His saying, the Exalted: **Thus does Allah clarify the signs for you so that you may reflect. Concerning the world and the Hereafter. And they ask you about the orphans...** (2:219-220)—the necessary implication would be that His saying "**We ordained for the Children of Israel...**" is the start of a new sentence. However, the established Quranic style in such cases is to use the conjunctive "and" (الواو) for a new sentence, as seen in the aforementioned verse of *al-Baqarah* and others.

As for the reason why "**From that account**" refers to the story of the two sons of Adam, it is because the story demonstrates that it is in the nature of this human species that following passion (اتباع الهوى) and envy (الحسد)—which is resentment toward people for things beyond their control—can drive a person, by the flimsiest of reasons, to contest the Lordship (الربوبية) and negate the purpose of creation by killing a brother of his own kind, even a full brother from the same father and mother.

Human individuals are but members of a single species and manifestations of a single reality. One of them carries the same humanity (الإنسانية) that many carry, and the whole carries what the part carries. Allah, the Glorified, only intended by creating individuals and multiplying progeny that this reality—which by its nature only lives for a short time—should endure and persist, such that the successor follows the predecessor and Allah is worshipped in His earth. Thus, the annihilation of an individual through murder is a corruption of creation (إفساد في الخلقة) and a negation of Allah's purpose for the humanity that is preserved through the multiplication of individuals by way of succession (الاستخلاف). This was pointed out by the murdered son of Adam when he addressed his brother: "**I will not stretch out my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.**" He indicated that unjust killing is a contestation of the Lordship.

Therefore, because it is in the nature of man to be driven by any flimsy reason to commit an injustice that, in reality, leads to the negation of the decree of Lordship and the purpose of creation regarding general humanity—and because the state of the Children of Israel was as Allah mentioned before these verses regarding envy, arrogance, following passion, and the rejection of truth—Allah explained to them the reality of this tragic injustice and its status according to precise measure. He informed them that the killing of one is, in His sight, equivalent to the killing of all, and conversely, the saving of one soul is, in His sight, equivalent to the saving of all.

Although this "ordaining" (الكتابة) does not contain a prescriptive legal ruling (حكم تكليفي), it nonetheless involves a severe emphasis in terms of status and consideration, and it has an effect in provoking divine wrath and indignation in this world or the Hereafter.

In brief: the meaning of the sentence is that since it is in the nature of man to be impelled by any flimsy reason to commit this great injustice, and since the affair of the Children of Israel was what it was, We explained to them the status of killing a soul so that they might refrain from excess (الإسراف). Yet, Our messengers came to them with clear proofs, but even after that, many of them are transgressors in the land.

As for His saying: "**that whoever kills a soul, without [its being retaliation for] a soul or for corruption [done] on the earth, it is as though he had killed all mankind**," the Exalted excepted the killing of a soul for a soul, which is the law of retaliation (القود) and retribution (القصاص) — *the legal principle of "an eye for an eye" in Islamic law* — as in His saying: "**Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain**" (2:178). He also excepted the killing of a soul for corruption on the earth, which is mentioned in the following verse: "**Indeed the retribution of those who fight Allah and His Apostle and spread corruption in the land...**" (5:33).

As for the status indicated by His saying "**it is as though**" (فَكَأَنَّما), it has been explained previously that a human individual, in terms of the reality he carries which lives and dies, carries humanity (الإنسانية), which is a single reality in all individuals, whether part or whole. One individual and many individuals are one in this regard. The necessary implication of this meaning is that the killing of a single soul is equivalent to the killing of the human species, and conversely, the saving of a single soul is equivalent to the saving of all mankind. This is what the noble verse conveys.

Two objections might be raised against the verse:
First: This comparison leads to a negation of the purpose. The purpose is to explain the gravity of murder in terms of sin and consequence. This implies that the gravity should increase as the number of killings increases. Equating one with all implies that nothing additional is accounted for beyond the first killing; if someone kills ten, the first would be like killing everyone, and the remaining nine would have nothing corresponding to them.

The objection is not resolved by saying that killing ten is equal to ten times killing everyone, or that killing everyone is equal to killing everyone multiplied by the number of everyone. This would mean a multiplication in the number of punishments, which the wording does not convey. Furthermore, the "all" is composed of individuals, each of whom is equal to the "all" composed of individuals, leading to an infinite regress. There is no meaning to "all" in this sense, for there is no single individual for it, and no "all" without individuals. Moreover, Allah says: "**Whoever brings an evil deed shall not be recompensed except with its like**" (6:160).

Second: If the killing of one being equal to the killing of all means the "all" that includes this one, it implies the equality of the part with the whole, which is self-evidently impossible. If it means the "all" excluding this one, it would mean: "whoever kills a soul, it is as if he killed other souls," which is a weak meaning that undermines the purpose of the speech, which is to show the ultimate gravity of this injustice. Furthermore, the absolute nature of His saying "**it is as though he had killed all mankind**" without exception rejects this possibility.

This objection is not resolved by claims that the equality refers to the punishment or the doubling of the torment, as is obvious.

The answer to both objections is that His saying "**whoever kills a soul... it is as though he had killed all mankind**" is a metonymy (كناية) for the fact that all people possess a single, united human reality in which the individual and the collective are equal. Whoever targets the humanity within one of them has targeted the humanity within all. It is like water distributed among many vessels; whoever drinks from one vessel has drunk water, and has targeted water qua water—and the water in all the vessels is no more than water qua water—so it is as if he drank it all. Thus, the sentence "**whoever kills...**" is a metonymy in the form of a simile (تشبيه), and both objections are resolved. Their basis was that the simile is a simple one where the point of comparison increases as the number of the compared objects increases. If equality were then made between one and all, the meaning would be corrupted and the objection would arise, as if one said: "One of the people is like one lion, and one of them is like all of them in power and bravery."

As for His saying, the Exalted: "**and whoever keeps it alive, it is as though he had kept all mankind alive**," the discussion regarding it is the same as the previous sentence. What is meant by "keeping alive" (الإحياء) is what is considered such in the custom of the rational, such as saving a drowning person or releasing a prisoner. Allah has also called guidance to the truth "giving life" in His speech; He said: "**Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people...**" (6:122). Thus, whoever guides a soul to faith has given it life.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "**Our messengers certainly brought them manifest proofs**," it is conjoined to the beginning of the verse, meaning: Our messengers came to them with clear proofs warning them against murder and all forms of corruption in the land that follow from it.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "**but even after that, many of them are transgressors in the land**," it completes the speech. By joining it, the intended purpose of the explanation is achieved: the realization that they are a corrupt people, persistent in their arrogance and rebellion. We explained to them the status of murder, and Our messengers came to them with clear proofs regarding it and other matters, explaining and warning them, yet they did not cease their persistence in rebellion and arrogance. They committed excess (الإسراف) in the land of old and continue to do so.

Excess (الإسراف) is to deviate from the middle path and exceed the limit in any action a person performs, though it is most commonly used in the context of spending, as in His saying: "**Those who, when they spend, are neither wasteful nor tightfisted, and there is a moderation between that**" (25:67), as mentioned by al-Raghib in *al-Mufradat*.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Hisham ibn Salim, from Habib al-Sijistani, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "When the two sons of Adam offered the sacrifice—and it was accepted from one of them and not from the other—he said: 'It was accepted from Abel (هابيل) and not from Cain (قابيل).' Intense envy entered him because of that, and he transgressed against Abel. He did not cease lying in wait for him and seeking his solitude until he caught him away from Adam, whereupon he pounced on him and killed him. Their story was as Allah has informed in His Book regarding the dialogue that took place between them before he killed him..." (the hadith).

I say: This narration is among the best narrated regarding the story. It is a long narration in which he (peace be upon him) mentions the birth of Seth (هبة الله) to Adam after that, his appointment as successor, and the course of succession among the prophets. We will quote it, Allah willing, in a suitable place. Its apparent meaning is that Cain killed Abel by treachery (غيلة) without Abel allowing him to do so, which is consistent with reason, as previously discussed in the commentary.

Know that the names of the two sons recorded in the narrations are Abel (هابيل) and Cain (قابيل), while in the current Torah they are Abel and Cain (قايين). There is no proof in the latter, as the chain of the Torah ends with an unknown person, in addition to its manifest alterations.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, it is said: My father narrated to us from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Hisham ibn Salim, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Thuwayr ibn Abi Fakhtah, who said: I heard Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) narrating to some men from the Quraysh: "When the two sons of Adam offered the sacrifice—one of them offered the fattest ram in his possession, and the other offered a bundle of grain—it was accepted from the owner of the ram, who was Abel, and not from the other. Cain became angry and said to Abel: 'By Allah, I will surely kill you.' Abel said: '**Allah only accepts from the God-conscious. Even if you stretch out your hand to kill me, I will not stretch out my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. I desire that you should bear my sin and your sin, and thus become one of the inmates of the Fire, and that is the reward of the wrongdoers.**'

Then his soul prompted him to kill his brother—but he did not know how to kill him until Iblis came and taught him, saying: 'Place his head between two stones and then crush it.' When he killed him, he did not know what to do with him. Then two crows came and began fighting until one killed the other. Then the survivor dug in the earth with its claws and buried its companion. Cain said: '**Woe to me! Am I unable to be like this crow and shroud my brother’s corpse?**' So he became one of the regretful. He dug a hole for him and buried him in it, and so it became a custom to bury the dead.

Cain returned to his father and Abel was not with him. Adam said to him: 'Where did you leave my son?' Cain said to him: 'Did you send me as a shepherd over him?' Adam said: 'Come with me to the place of the sacrifice.' Adam's soul sensed what Cain had done. When he reached the place of the sacrifice, his murder became clear to him. Adam cursed the earth that accepted Abel's blood, and Adam ordered that Cain be cursed. Cain was called from the heaven: 'You are cursed just as you killed your brother.' For this reason, the earth does not drink blood.

Adam went away weeping for Abel for forty days and nights. When his grief became intense, he complained of that to Allah. Allah revealed to him: 'I am granting you a male child who will be a successor to Abel.' Eve gave birth to a pure, blessed boy. On the seventh day, Allah revealed to him: 'O Adam, this boy is a gift (هبة) from Me to you, so name him Hibatullah (هبة الله).' So Adam named him Hibatullah."

I say: This narration is of average quality among those narrated regarding the story and its aftermath. Nevertheless, it is not free from some confusion in its text, as its apparent meaning is that Cain threatened Abel with death but then did not know how to kill him. This is inconceivable unless it means he was confused as to which method of killing to choose, so Iblis—may Allah curse him—suggested crushing his head with stones. There are other narrations from both Sunni and Shi'a sources whose content is similar to this one.

Know that there are many narrations regarding the story with varying and strange contents. Some say that Allah took Abel's ram and stored it in Paradise for forty autumns, then it became the ransom for Ishmael when Abraham sacrificed him. Others say that Abel allowed Cain to kill him and refrained from stretching his hand toward his brother. Some say that when Cain killed his brother, Allah shackled one of his legs to his thigh from the day of the murder until the Day of Resurrection, and turned his face to the right; wherever he turns, a pen of snow surrounds him in winter and a pen of fire in summer, and seven angels are with him—whenever one leaves, another comes. Others say he is punished on an island in the sea, hung upside down until the Day of Resurrection. Some say Cain is suspended by his horns in the eye of the sun, turning with it in its freezing cold and boiling heat until the Day of Resurrection, and then Allah will cast him into the Fire. Some say the son of Adam who killed his brother was Cain who was born in Paradise. Others say that when Abel's murder became clear to Adam, he elegized him with several verses in Arabic. Some say it was part of their law that if someone intended to harm another, the latter should let him do as he wished without resisting. There are many other such narrations.

These and their like are narrations whose chains are mostly or entirely weak. They do not accord with sound reason, nor does the Book agree with them. They are either clearly fabricated, or distorted, or cases where the narrators erred in transmitting the meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Umar that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Is any of you unable, when a man comes to kill him, to say like this?"—and he placed one hand over the other—"so that he may be like the better of the two sons of Adam? He will be in Paradise and his killer will be in the Fire."

I say: This is among the narrations concerning tribulations (الفتن), which are many. Al-Suyuti narrated most of them in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, such as what he narrated from al-Bayhaqi from Abu Musa from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Break your swords"—meaning during tribulation—"cut your bowstrings, and stay in the depths of your houses, and be therein like the better of the two sons of Adam." And what he narrated from Ibn Jarir and Abd al-Razzaq from al-Hasan: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The two sons of Adam were made an example for this community, so take the better of the two."

These narrations, in their apparent meaning, do not harmonize with sound reason supported by authentic reports commanding self-defense and the defense of truth. Allah, the Exalted, said: **If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them. But if one of them aggresses against the other, fight the one which aggresses until it returns to Allah’s ordinance.** (49:9).

Furthermore, they all interpret the saying of Abel quoted in the story: "**Even if you stretch out your hand to kill me, I will not stretch out my hand toward you to kill you**" as meaning that Abel allowed his brother to kill him and abandoned defense. You have already seen the problems with this.

What further raises suspicion about them is that they are narrated by people who sat out the tribulation of the House [the siege of Uthman] and the wars of Ali (peace be upon him) with Mu'awiyah, the Kharijites, Talhah, and al-Zubayr. It is necessary to interpret them in a certain way if possible, otherwise they must be rejected.
