In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "In Damascus there is a mountain called Qasiyun; there the son of Adam killed his brother."

I say: There is no harm in this narration, except that Ibn Asakir narrated through a chain from Ka'b al-Ahbar that he said: "The blood on Mount Qasiyun is the blood of the son of Adam." Through another chain from Amr ibn Khabir al-Sha'bani: "I was with Ka'b al-Ahbar on Mount Dayr al-Murran and he saw a flowing stream on the mountain and said: 'Here the son of Adam killed his brother, and this is the trace of his blood which Allah made a sign for the worlds.'"

These two reports indicate that there was a permanent trace there claimed to be the blood of the murdered Abel. It is likely that this is among the superstitious matters they fabricated to direct people toward visiting it, making vows, and giving gifts, similar to the manufactured handprints and footprints on stones, the "Grave of the Grandmother" [Eve], and so on.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "No soul is killed unjustly except that a portion of its blood is upon the first son of Adam, because he was the first to establish the custom of killing."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through Sunni and Shi'a sources via other chains as well.

In *al-Kafi*, through its chain from Humran, who said: I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "What is the meaning of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: '**From that account We ordained for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul, without [its being retaliation for] a soul or for corruption [done] on the earth, it is as though he had killed all mankind**'?" I said: "How is it as though he killed all mankind when he only killed one?" He said: "He is placed in a location in Hell to which the ultimate intensity of the punishment of its inhabitants reaches; had he killed all mankind, he would only have entered that place." I said: "What if he kills another?" He said: "It is doubled for him." I say: Al-Saduq narrated the same in *Ma'ani al-Akhbar* from Humran.

His question "What if he kills another?" refers to the previously explained objection regarding the necessary equality between killing one and killing one plus others. He (peace be upon him) answered it by saying "It is doubled for him." The objection that he abandoned the equality indicated by the verse of status—"**whoever kills a soul...**"—does not apply. While doubling implies that one and many are not equal, the equality of status refers to the *type* of punishment, meaning that the killer of one, two, or all is in the same valley among the valleys of Hell. This is indicated by his (peace be upon him) saying in the narration: "Had he killed all mankind, he would only have entered that place."

Evidence for what we have mentioned is found in what al-Ayyashi narrated in his *Tafsir* from Humran from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "It is a status in the Fire to which the ultimate intensity of the punishment of all the inhabitants of the Fire reaches, so he is placed in it." I said: "Even if he killed two?" He said: "Do you not see that there is no status in the Fire more severe in punishment than it? It is doubled for him according to what he did." The combination of negation and affirmation in his (peace be upon him) answer is only for the reason we have explained: that the unity and equality are in the *type* of punishment—to which the "status" refers—while the difference is in the specific degree and the actual torment the killer tastes within it.

This is also supported in general by what is narrated from Hanan ibn Sadir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: "**whoever kills a soul... it is as though he had killed all mankind**": "It is a valley in Hell; had he killed all mankind he would be in it, and if he killed a single soul he would be in it."

I say: It seems the verse is quoted there by its meaning.

In *al-Kafi*, through its chain from Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in His Book: "**and whoever keeps it alive, it is as though he had kept all mankind alive**." He said: "[Saving] from burning or drowning." I said: "What about one who brings it out from misguidance to guidance?" He said: "That is its greatest interpretation (تأويلها الأعظم)."

I say: Al-Shaykh narrated it in his *Amali* and al-Barqi in *al-Mahasin* from Fudayl from him (peace be upon him). The hadith is also narrated from Sama'ah and Humran from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him).

The meaning of saving from misguidance being the "greatest interpretation" of the verse is that it is its most profound explanation. The term *ta'wil* (التأويل) — *the deeper meaning or interpretation of a text* — was often used in early Islam as a synonym for *tafsir* (التفسير) — *the exegesis or explanation of the Quran*.

This is supported by what is in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi* from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): I asked him about the saying of Allah: "**whoever kills a soul, without [its being retaliation for] a soul or for corruption [done] on the earth, it is as though he had killed all mankind**." He said: "He has a seat in the Fire; had he killed all mankind, the punishment would not exceed that." He said: "**and whoever keeps it alive, it is as though he had kept all mankind alive**—he did not kill it, or he saved it from drowning or burning, and greater than all of that is bringing it out from misguidance to guidance."

I say: His saying "he did not kill it" means he did not kill it after the right to kill it was established, as in the case of retribution.

In the same source, from Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): I asked him about "**and whoever keeps it alive, it is as though he had kept all mankind alive**." He said: "Whoever brings it out from disbelief to faith."

I say: This meaning has been reported in many narrations from Sunni sources as well.

In *al-Majma'*: It is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "The transgressors (المسرفون) are those who deem the forbidden lawful and shed blood."

## (Scientific and Comparative Discussion)

In the fourth chapter of the Book of Genesis in the Torah, it states: "(1) And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the Lord. (2) And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground. (3) And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the Lord. (4) And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the Lord had respect unto Abel and to his offering: (5) But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell. (6) And the Lord said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen? (7) If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him. (8) And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him. (9) And the Lord said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper? (10) And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground. (11) And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand; (12) When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth. (13) And Cain said unto the Lord, My punishment is greater than I can bear. (14) Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me. (15) And the Lord said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the Lord set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him. (16) And Cain went out from the presence of the Lord, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden." (End of quote).

What is in the Quran regarding their story is His saying, the Exalted: **Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons: when the two of them offered an offering, it was accepted from one of them and not accepted from the other. [One] said, ‘I will surely kill you.’ [The other] said, ‘Allah accepts only from the God-conscious. (27) Even if you stretch out your hand to kill me, I will not stretch out my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (28) I desire that you should bear my sin and your sin, and thus become one of the inmates of the Fire, and that is the reward of the wrongdoers.’ (29) Then his soul prompted him to kill his brother, and he killed him, and thus became one of the losers. (30) Then Allah sent a crow, scavenging in the earth, to show him how to shroud his brother’s corpse. He said, ‘Woe to me! Am I unable to be like this crow and shroud my brother’s corpse?’ So he became one of the regretful.** (5:27-31).

You should reflect upon what the story contains as narrated by the Torah and as narrated by the Quran, then compare them and judge as you will.

The first thing that appears to you from the Torah is that it makes the Lord, the Exalted, an earthly being in the form of a human who associates with people, judging for and against them as one of them would judge. He is approached, drawn near to, and spoken to as one person does with another. Then one is hidden from Him by distance and absence, so He does not see the distant and absent as He sees the near and present. In short, His state is that of an earthly human in all respects, except that His will is effective when He wills and His judgment is binding when He judges. Upon this foundation all the teachings of the Torah and the Gospel are built. Exalted is Allah far above that.

The necessary implication of the story therein is that mankind lived in those days in a state of direct address and presence before Allah, the Glorified, then He became veiled from Cain, or from him and his like, while others remained in their state. This is despite the fact that definitive proofs establish that mankind is a single species with similar individuals living a worldly, material life, and that Allah, majestic is His station, is transcendent above being described with material attributes and states, and is sanctified above the accidents of contingency and the occurrences of deficiency and change. This is what the Quran explains.

As for the Quran, it narrates the story on the basis of the similarity of individuals, but it concludes the story of the murder with the story of the sending of the crow. Thus, it reveals the reality that man is gradual in his perfection, building his progress in the stages of vital perfection upon the foundation of sense and thought.

Then it mentions the dialogue between the two brothers, narrating from the murdered one the finest of human primordial insights and the foundations of religious knowledge—monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد). Then it mentions the matter of godwariness (التقوى) and injustice (الظلم), which are the two active principles in all divine laws and legal rulings, followed by divine justice in the matter of acceptance, rejection, and otherworldly recompense.

Then it mentions the regret of the killer after his deed and his loss in this world and the Hereafter. After all this, it explains that the nature of murder is such that what occurs against a single soul is like what occurs against all mankind, and that whoever keeps a soul alive, it is as though he kept all mankind alive.
